content.problemStatement:
Mr. Wire Less is not that good at implementing circuits on a breadboard. In his Digital Logic Design course, he has to implement several boolean functions using the breadboard. In a breadboard, inputs are given through switches and outputs are taken through LEDs. Each input switch can be either in the ground state or in the high state. So, if he wishes to implement a boolean function, f(x1,x2,…,xn)f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)f(x1​,x2​,…,xn​) that takes nnn boolean values as input and returns mmm boolean values as output, he will use nnn switches and mmm LEDs.

Mr. Wire Less can quickly assemble the necessary ICs and wires, but the key problem is testing. While testing, he has to check with all possible input combinations to ensure that the output of the LED corresponds to the expected output. This is taking too long for him, as most of the switches are jammed and difficult to toggle.

Mr. Wire Less is asking for help to minimize his time of testing. So, your task is to minimize the total number of switch toggles throughout the testing.

For example, if Mr. Wire Less has to test a function f(x0,x1)f(x_0, x_1)f(x0​,x1​) of two variables, he may choose this switching sequence for testing: 00,11,10,0100, 11, 10, 0100,11,10,01. In this case, the total number of switch toggles will be 2+1+2=52 + 1 + 2 = 52+1+2=5. But if he tests in this sequence: 00,10,11,0100, 10, 11, 0100,10,11,01, the total number of toggles will be 1+1+1=31 + 1 + 1 = 31+1+1=3.

Given nnn, you have to output the minimum number of toggles needed for complete testing. Though it seems impractical, he wants you to solve the problem for a very large value of nnn. But, then the toggle value can be quite big. So, he is completely okay with the toggle value modulo 8589934592(233)8589934592 (2^{33})8589934592(233).


Input Format
The first line of the input contains a positive integer T(T≤105)T (T \leq 10^5)T(T≤105), denoting the number of test cases. Each of the following TTT lines contains a single non-negative integer n(n≤1020)n (n \leq 10^{20})n(n≤1020).



Output Format
For every test case, output a single line containing the test case number and the minimum number of switch toggles modulo 8589934592(233)8589934592 (2^{33})8589934592(233).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a positive integer T, denoting the number of test cases. Each of the following T lines contains a single non-negative integer n.
content.outputFormat: For every test case, output a single line containing the test case number and the minimum number of switch toggles modulo 8589934592 (2^33).

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, when n = 1, the minimum number of toggles required is 1. For the second test case, when n = 2, the minimum number of toggles required is 3.

solution.approach: The minimum number of switch toggles required is equal to the number of bits set in the binary representation of 2^n - 1. This is because each bit set in the binary representation corresponds to a switch toggle. We can use the property of modular arithmetic to calculate the result modulo 2^33.
solution.timeComplexity: O(log n) — because we are calculating the binary representation of 2^n - 1, which has at most log n bits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) — because we are using a constant amount of space to store the result.

code.Python3:
import sys

T = int(sys.stdin.readline())
for t in range(T):
    n = int(sys.stdin.readline())
    result = (1 << n) - 1
    print(f"Case {t + 1}: {result % (1 << 33)}")
